Veteran Resources

Everything You Need to
Navigate Your TDIU Claim

Free guides, explainers, and tools to help veterans understand the VA TDIU process — and how a vocational evaluation can strengthen your case.

Guides & Tools

Veteran Resource Library

Practical, plain-language resources covering every stage of the TDIU process.

Getting Started

What Is VA TDIU?

Total Disability based on Individual Unemployability (TDIU) allows veterans rated less than 100% disabled to receive full disability compensation if their service-connected conditions prevent gainful employment.

Learn More →
Eligibility

Do I Qualify for TDIU?

You may qualify with a single disability rated at 60%+, or multiple disabilities with a combined rating of 70%+ (with at least one at 40%). Vocational evidence can strengthen borderline cases significantly.

Check Eligibility →
The Process

How a Vocational Evaluation Helps

A vocational expert documents how your specific service-connected limitations affect your ability to maintain substantially gainful employment — providing the critical bridge between medical evidence and VA decision-makers.

Get Evaluated →
VA Forms

Key VA Forms for TDIU

VA Form 21-8940 (Veteran's Application for Increased Compensation Based on Unemployability) is the primary form. VA Form 21-4192 documents your employment history. We help you understand what supporting evidence to gather.

Get Help →
Appeals

Denied? Here's What to Do

A denial isn't the end. A strong vocational evaluation can be powerful new evidence in an appeal under the AMA (Appeals Modernization Act). Supplemental claims with expert vocational opinions have a strong success record.

Discuss Your Appeal →
Attorney Partnership

Working With a VA Attorney

Veterans law attorneys and accredited claims agents often recommend independent vocational evaluations to substantiate TDIU claims. Learn how Vocemploy works alongside your legal representation.

Attorney Info →
Common Questions

Frequently Asked Questions

The VA generally defines substantially gainful employment as work that pays above the federal poverty threshold. If your service-connected conditions prevent you from consistently working at this level, you may be eligible for TDIU — even if you can do some part-time or marginal work.

Vocemploy typically completes vocational evaluations within 2–3 weeks of receiving necessary documentation. The overall VA claims process varies, but having a thorough vocational evaluation upfront helps avoid requests for additional evidence that slow down decisions.

Not always, but a vocational evaluation can still be valuable. If you have a combined rating between 70–99% and cannot work, an independent vocational evaluation provides expert documentation that often makes the difference between approval and denial — especially for borderline cases.

TDIU recipients can engage in "marginal employment" — generally defined as earning below the federal poverty level. However, substantially gainful employment may result in termination of TDIU benefits. We recommend consulting with a VA-accredited attorney about your specific situation.

Helpful documents include: VA rating decision letters, service medical records, private medical records related to service-connected conditions, employment history (VA Form 21-4192), and any previous vocational or medical opinions. Vocemploy will guide you through exactly what's needed during your free consultation.

VA Vocational Rehabilitation (Chapter 31) helps veterans prepare for employment. Vocemploy provides independent vocational evaluations specifically to support TDIU disability claims — documenting why you cannot maintain gainful employment. These are complementary but very different services.

Key Terms

TDIU Glossary

Plain-language definitions of the terms you'll encounter in your VA claim.

TDIU
Total Disability based on Individual Unemployability. A VA benefit that pays at the 100% rate when service-connected disabilities prevent substantially gainful employment.
Vocational Evaluation
A professional assessment of a veteran's work capacity, skills, and employment limitations — used as evidence in VA disability claims.
Combined Rating
The VA's method of calculating total disability from multiple conditions. It is not simply additive — a veteran with two 50% ratings does not have a 100% combined rating.
Substantially Gainful Employment
Work that pays above the federal poverty threshold and is performed with reasonable regularity. The inability to perform such work is the key criterion for TDIU.
VA Form 21-8940
Veteran's Application for Increased Compensation Based on Unemployability. The primary form used to apply for TDIU benefits.
AMA (Appeals Modernization Act)
The 2019 law that restructured the VA appeals process, creating three review lanes: Supplemental Claim, Higher-Level Review, and Board of Veterans' Appeals.
Marginal Employment
Work that pays at or below the federal poverty level. Veterans receiving TDIU can generally engage in marginal employment without affecting their benefits.
Service-Connected Condition
A disability or health condition the VA has determined was caused or aggravated by military service. Only service-connected conditions count toward TDIU eligibility.
